source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.5 Link files (pp. 149-150)

questions.q: Create a SOFT (symbolic) link named /root/slink1 pointing to /root/Afile.
questions.why: ln -s makes a symbolic link; the target comes first, the link name second.

questions.q: Create a HARD link named /home/jack/read to the existing file /home/paul/letter.
questions.why: ln with no option creates a hard link - a second name mapping the same inode.

questions.q: What happens to a soft (symbolic) link when its original file is deleted?
options: The link breaks - it still exists but points to a file that is gone
The link is automatically deleted too
The link becomes a hard link
questions.why: A soft link is just a pointer to the original path (like a Windows shortcut); delete the original and the link dangles.

questions.q: What is the defining feature of a hard link?
options: It shares the SAME inode number as the original, so both names are the same physical file
It has a different inode and a copy of the data
It is always shown in red by ls
It only works across different file systems
questions.why: Hard links are different directory entries for the same inode; content, owner and timestamps stay in sync because it is one file.

questions.q: What are the restrictions on hard links?
options: They cannot span partitions/file systems and cannot be used on directories
They cannot point to ordinary files
They may only be created by root
They break when the original is edited
questions.why: Because they rely on a shared inode, hard links can't cross file systems, and 'ln /etc /root/etc_hardlink' fails with 'hard link not allowed for directory'.

questions.q: How do you tell ln to make a soft link rather than a hard link?
options: Add the -s option (ln without -s makes a hard link)
questions.why: ln -s creates a symbolic link; plain ln creates a hard link.
